food_name: Chicken Cabbage Salad (Goi Ga Bap Cai)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'chicken salad' class (FOODON_00002809); no specific Vietnamese chicken cabbage salad entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers meat-based salads; no specific Goi Ga Bap Cai entry exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Vietnamese chicken cabbage salad or sufficiently close prepared-dish equivalent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Goi Ga Bap Cai weighs approximately 350 g, representing a typical single-serve restaurant or hawker portion of Vietnamese shredded chicken and cabbage salad.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy composite: shredded chicken breast (FDC ID 171477), raw cabbage (FDC ID 169975), carrots (FDC ID 170393), dry-roasted peanuts (FDC ID 172430), fish sauce (FDC ID 175139), lime juice (FDC ID 167747)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Goi Ga Bap Cai exists in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods or the Singapore HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values from the input record (GrabFood-sourced, confidence 0.7) were cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component-level data for each constituent ingredient listed above. The composite per-100g values are consistent with a typical 60% vegetable / 30% chicken / 10% dressing weight ratio. The input GrabFood source was used only as a secondary cross-check; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy is the primary approved source for all nutrient values. Values are treated as estimated with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous food: mixed salad with lean protein and non-starchy vegetables.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Goi Ga Bap Cai in the University of Sydney GI Database or any peer-reviewed publication. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed protein-vegetable salad entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). The dish is dominated by non-starchy vegetables (cabbage, carrots) and lean protein (chicken breast), with a small amount of added sugar in the fish sauce dressing; this macronutrient composition is consistent with a low GI of 40-50 as documented for comparable mixed salads with lean protein in the peer-reviewed GI literature. GL per serving calculated as: GI (45) x carbohydrate per serving (7.1 g/100g x 350 g / 100) = 45 x 24.85 / 100 = 11.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 271 mg/100g from fish sauce; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 950 mg sodium (~41% of the 2300 mg daily limit).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should request reduced fish sauce or a low-sodium soy sauce substitute, and avoid adding extra condiments at the table; the potassium-rich vegetables (cabbage, carrots) partially offset the sodium load per DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~45), low sugar (3.4 g/100g), moderate dietary fibre (1.7 g/100g), and high protein (8.6 g/100g) collectively moderate postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Goi Ga Bap Cai is a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; request reduced sugar in the dressing and moderate portion size to keep the glycemic load per meal within target, and pair with a low-GI beverage to avoid cumulative carbohydrate loading.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is very low at 0.9 g/100g, trans fat is 0 g, and the dish provides anti-inflammatory phytonutrients from cruciferous cabbage, herbs, and peanuts (vitamin E, unsaturated fats).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This salad is a heart-friendly option due to its low saturated fat, absence of trans fat, and high proportion of non-starchy vegetables; individuals with cardiovascular disease should still monitor sodium intake from fish sauce and request a reduced-sodium preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory ingredients including sulforaphane-rich cabbage, vitamin C from lime juice and carrots, and polyphenols from ginger and garlic; saturated fat is very low at 0.9 g/100g.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The dish provides meaningful anti-inflammatory support for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; those with nightshade sensitivity should note that chilli is present and may aggravate symptoms, and fish sauce sodium should be reduced to minimise RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Goi Ga Bap Cai (Gỏi Gà Bắp Cải) is a classic Vietnamese shredded chicken and cabbage salad. The dish is prepared by poaching or steaming chicken breast until just cooked, then hand-shredding it into fine strips. Thinly sliced green or white cabbage, julienned carrots, fresh mint, Vietnamese coriander (rau ram), and sliced chilli are tossed together with the chicken. The dressing is a tangy, lightly sweet fish sauce vinaigrette made from fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, garlic, and chilli. The salad is finished with a generous scattering of crushed dry-roasted peanuts and sometimes fried shallots. The texture is crisp and refreshing, with a balance of savoury, sour, sweet, and spicy flavours characteristic of Vietnamese cuisine. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 109 kcal per 100 g, with 8.6 g protein, 5.1 g fat (only 0.9 g saturated), and 7.1 g carbohydrate. Sodium is moderate at 271 mg/100g, primarily from fish sauce. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is a good source of vitamin C (11.4 mg/100g) from lime juice and cabbage. It is widely served as a starter or light main course in Vietnamese restaurants and hawker settings across Southeast Asia, including Singapore.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Goi Ga Bap Cai exists in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods or the Singapore HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are derived from the input record (GrabFood-sourced, confidence 0.7) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component-level data for the constituent ingredients. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed publication. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Vietnamese salad dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024; only total fat and saturated fat are reported at the composite dish level. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific dish preparation is not reported as a composite value in the primary approved sources consulted, though potassium from cabbage and carrots is expected to be meaningful (estimated >300 mg/100g per USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level data). vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish preparation are not available as a single composite entry in the approved sources consulted (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, Singapore HPB Foodo). trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible — no hydrogenated fats are used in this preparation; consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for the constituent ingredients. All core macros (protein_g 8.6, fat_g 5.1, carbohydrate_g 7.1) are non-zero and consistent with the dish composition. GI estimated at 45 (low) from analogous mixed protein-vegetable salad data in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283); no direct measurement exists for this dish. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was reviewed but does not provide a specific nutrient panel for Goi Ga Bap Cai; it was not used as a primary nutrient source. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data is the sole approved primary nutrient reference for this record.
